docs.title: DBWarden - Professional Database Migration System
docs.text: DBWarden is a professional-grade database migration system designed specifically for Python projects using SQLAlchemy. It provides a robust, reliable, and developer-friendly approach to managing database schema changes across different environments.

docs.title: What is DBWarden?
docs.text: DBWarden addresses the critical challenge of maintaining consistent database schemas across development, staging, and production environments. It combines the flexibility of SQL-based migrations with the convenience of automatic migration generation from SQLAlchemy models.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Automatic Migration Generation: Automatically generate SQL migrations from your SQLAlchemy models
  • Version Control: Track all schema changes with detailed history and timestamps
  • Rollback Support: Safely revert migrations when needed
  • Multiple Database Support: Works with PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQLite
  • Model Discovery: Automatically finds and processes your SQLAlchemy models
  • Migration Locking: Prevents concurrent migration execution
  • Schema Inspection: Inspect and compare database schemas

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text:
DBWarden follows a modular architecture designed for reliability and extensibility:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     DBWarden CLI                        │
│                    (Typer-based)                        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Commands Layer                                         │
│  ├── init         ├── migrate      ├── rollback         │
│  ├── make-migrations ├── history    ├── status          │
│  └── ...          └── ...           └── ...             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Engine Layer                                           │
│  ├── Model Discovery  ├── Versioning  ├── File Parser   │
│  └── Checksum         └── Locking                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Repository Layer                                       │
│  ├── Migration Records  ├── Lock Management             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Database Layer                                         │
│  ├── Connection Pool  ├── SQL Execution                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
# Initialize migrations directory
dbwarden init

# Generate migration from SQLAlchemy models
dbwarden make-migrations "create users table"

# Apply migrations
dbwarden migrate --verbose

# Check migration history
dbwarden history

docs.title: Migration Locking
docs.text: DBWarden uses a locking mechanism to prevent concurrent migration execution.

docs.title: Automatic Locking
docs.text: Locks are automatically acquired and released during: - migrate - rollback

docs.title: Manual Lock Management
docs.text:
Check lock status:

dbwarden lock-status

Force unlock (emergency):

dbwarden unlock

docs.title: Lock Implementation
docs.text:
-- dbwarden_lock table
CREATE TABLE dbwarden_lock (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    locked BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
    acquired_at DATETIME,
    release_at DATETIME
);

docs.title: Checksum Validation
docs.text: DBWarden validates migration integrity using checksums.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Before execution: Calculate checksum of migration file
  2. After execution: Store checksum in database
  3. Future runs: Verify checksum matches stored value

docs.title: Checksum Algorithm
docs.text:
Uses SHA-256 hash of SQL statements:

import hashlib

def calculate_checksum(sql_statements):
    content = "".join(sql_statements)
    return hashlib.sha256(content.encode()).hexdigest()

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
On migration run:

stored_checksum = get_stored_checksum()
current_checksum = calculate_checksum(sql_statements)

if stored_checksum != current_checksum:
    raise MigrationChecksumError("Migration file has been modified!")

docs.title: Squash Migrations
docs.text: Combine multiple migrations into one.

docs.title: When to Squash
docs.text:
• After 10+ small migrations
  • Before major releases
  • During maintenance windows

docs.title: Process
docs.text:
# 1. Ensure all migrations applied
dbwarden migrate

# 2. Squash migrations
dbwarden squash

# 3. New migration created
ls migrations/
# 0001_initial_schema.sql  (consolidated)

docs.text: Paths are processed in order: 1. First path's models processed first 2. Duplicate tables (same __tablename__) are skipped after first occurrence

docs.text:
All migrations run in specified schema:

CREATE TABLE my_schema.users (... Multiple Schemas

For multiple);

docs.title: schemas, use fully qualified names in migrations:
docs.text:
-- upgrade

CREATE TABLE analytics.events (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(200)
);

CREATE TABLE public.users (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    email VARCHAR(255)
);

-- rollback

DROP TABLE public.users;
DROP TABLE analytics.events;

docs.title: Basic Transaction
docs.text:
-- upgrade

BEGIN;

CREATE TABLE orders (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    total DECIMAL(10, 2)
);

CREATE TABLE order_items (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    order_id INTEGER REFERENCES orders(id)
);

COMMIT;

-- rollback

BEGIN;

DROP TABLE order_items;
DROP TABLE orders;

COMMIT;

docs.title: Complex Migration Pattern
docs.text:
-- upgrade

-- Step 1: Create new table with new structure
CREATE TABLE posts_new (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    author_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    title VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
    content TEXT,
    slug VARCHAR(200) UNIQUE,
    created_at DATETIME DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

-- Step 2: Copy data with transformation
INSERT INTO posts_new (id, author_id, title, content, slug, created_at)
SELECT 
    id, 
    user_id, 
    title, 
    content, 
    LOWER(REPLACE(title, ' ', '-')),
    created_at
FROM posts;

-- Step 3: Drop old table
DROP TABLE posts;

-- Step 4: Rename new table
ALTER TABLE posts_new RENAME TO posts;

-- rollback

-- Reverse all steps
CREATE TABLE posts_old (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    title VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
    content TEXT,
    created_at DATETIME DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

INSERT INTO posts_old (id, user_id, title, content, created_at)
SELECT id, author_id, title, content, created_at FROM posts;

DROP TABLE posts;

ALTER TABLE posts_old RENAME TO posts;

docs.title: GitHub Actions
docs.text:
name: Database Migrations

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      target:
        description: 'Target version to migrate to'
        required: false

jobs:
  migrate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v4
        with:
          python-version: '3.11'

      - name: Install DBWarden
        run: |
          pip install dbwarden

      - name: Run Migrations
        run: |
          if [ "${{ github.event.inputs.target }}" ]; then
            dbwarden migrate --to-version ${{ github.event.inputs.target }}
          else
            dbwarden migrate --verbose
          fi
        env:
          DBWARDEN_SQLALCHEMY_URL: ${{ secrets.DATABASE_URL }}
          DBWARDEN_ASYNC: ${{ secrets.DBWARDEN_ASYNC || 'false' }}

docs.title: Docker Integration
docs.text:
FROM python:3.11-slim

WORKDIR /app

COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

COPY . .

RUN pip install dbwarden

# Run migrations on container start
CMD ["sh", "-c", "dbwarden migrate && your_app"]

docs.title: Kubernetes
docs.text:
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: dbwarden-migrate
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: migrate
        image: your-app:latest
        command: ["dbwarden", "migrate", "--verbose"]
        env:
        - name: DBWARDEN_SQLALCHEMY_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: db-credentials
              key: url
      restartPolicy: OnFailure

docs.title: Using Migration Tags
docs.text:
-- 0001_base_schema.sql (always run)

-- upgrade
CREATE TABLE users (id INT PRIMARY KEY, email VARCHAR(255));
-- rollback
DROP TABLE users;

-- 0002_demo_data.sql (demo only)

-- upgrade
INSERT INTO users (email) VALUES ('demo@example.com');
-- rollback
DELETE FROM users WHERE email = 'demo@example.com';

docs.title: Programmatic Execution
docs.text:
from dbwarden.config import get_config
from dbwarden.commands.migrate import migrate_cmd

# Apply migrations with target version
migrate_cmd(to_version="0001", verbose=True)

docs.title: Batch Migrations
docs.text:
For many small migrations:

# Apply all pending
dbwarden migrate

# Or apply specific number
dbwarden migrate --count 10

docs.title: Parallel Checks (Future)
docs.text: Currently migrations run sequentially for safety.

docs.title: After Failed Migration
docs.text:
1. Check lock status
  2. Unlock if necessary
  3. Verify database state
  4. Fix migration file
  5. Re-apply
dbwarden lock-status
dbwarden unlock  # if locked
dbwarden history
dbwarden status
# Fix migration file
dbwarden migrate

docs.title: Manual State Correction
docs.text:
In emergencies, manually update migration records:

-- Remove last migration record
DELETE FROM dbwarden_migrations
WHERE version = '0001'
ORDER BY applied_at DESC
LIMIT 1;

docs.title: Best Practices Summary
docs.text: Practice Description Idempotent migrations Safe to run multiple times Include rollback Always have a rollback plan Test migrations On staging before production Backup first Especially in production Version control Commit migration files Review SQL Before applying Document complex migrations Comment your SQL Use transactions For multi-step changes

docs.title: Pattern 1: Safe Column Addition
docs.text:
-- upgrade
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN bio TEXT;
UPDATE users SET bio = '' WHERE bio IS NULL;
ALTER TABLE users ALTER COLUMN bio SET NOT NULL;

-- rollback
ALTER TABLE users ALTER COLUMN bio DROP NOT NULL;
ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN bio;

docs.title: Pattern 2: Safe Data Migration
docs.text:
-- upgrade
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN new_email VARCHAR(255);

UPDATE users SET new_email = email;

-- AFTER VERIFICATION:
-- ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN email;
-- ALTER TABLE users RENAME COLUMN new_email TO email;

docs.title: Pattern 3: Version-Based Feature Flags
docs.text:
-- upgrade
CREATE TABLE feature_flags (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE,
    enabled BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE
);

INSERT INTO feature_flags (name, enabled) VALUES ('new_checkout', FALSE);

-- rollback
DROP TABLE feature_flags;

docs.title: init
docs.text:
Initialize the migrations directory.

dbwarden init

No arguments or options.

docs.title: make-migrations
docs.text:
Auto-generate SQL migration from SQLAlchemy models.

dbwarden make-migrations [DESCRIPTION] [OPTIONS]

Arguments: - DESCRIPTION: Description for the migration (optional)

Options: - -v, --verbose: Enable verbose logging

Examples:

dbwarden make-migrations "create users table"
dbwarden make-migrations "add posts" --verbose
dbwarden make-migrations

docs.title: new
docs.text:
Create a new manual migration file.

dbwarden new DESCRIPTION [OPTIONS]

Arguments: - DESCRIPTION: Description of the migration (required)

Options: - --version VERSION, -v VERSION: Version number for the migration (optional)

Examples:

dbwarden new "add index to users email"
dbwarden new "custom migration" --version 2.0.0

docs.title: migrate
docs.text:
Apply pending migrations to the database.

dbwarden migrate [OPTIONS]

Options: - -c, --count COUNT: Number of migrations to apply (optional) - -t, --to-version VERSION: Migrate to a specific version (optional) - -v, --verbose: Enable verbose logging (optional)

Examples:

dbwarden migrate
dbwarden migrate --verbose
dbwarden migrate --count 2
dbwarden migrate --to-version 0003
dbwarden migrate -c 1 -t 0002 -v

docs.title: rollback
docs.text:
Rollback the last applied migration.

dbwarden rollback [OPTIONS]

Options: - -c, --count COUNT: Number of migrations to rollback (optional) - -t, --to-version VERSION: Rollback to a specific version (optional) - -v, --verbose: Enable verbose logging (optional)

Examples:

dbwarden rollback
dbwarden rollback --verbose
dbwarden rollback --count 2
dbwarden rollback --to-version 0001
dbwarden rollback -c 1 -t 0001 -v

docs.title: squash
docs.text:
Merge multiple consecutive migrations into one.

dbwarden squash [OPTIONS]

Options: - -v, --verbose: Enable verbose logging (optional)

Example:

dbwarden squash
dbwarden squash --verbose

docs.text:

dbwarden history

No arguments or options.

docs.text:

docs.title: version
docs.text:

docs.title: check-db
docs.text:
Inspect the live database schema.

dbwarden check-db [OPTIONS]

Options: - -o, --out FORMAT: Output format (json, yaml, sql, txt) (optional, default: txt)

Examples:

dbwarden check-db
dbwarden check-db --out json
dbwarden check-db --out yaml
dbwarden check-db -o sql

docs.title: diff
docs.text:
Show structural differences between models and database.

dbwarden diff [TYPE] [OPTIONS]

Arguments: - TYPE: Type of diff - one of: models, migrations, all (optional, default: all)

Options: - -v, --verbose: Enable verbose logging (optional)

Examples:

dbwarden diff
dbwarden diff --verbose
dbwarden diff models
dbwarden diff migrations -v

docs.text: Enable verbose logging with SQL syntax highlighting. Available on: - make-migrations - migrate - rollback - squash - diff

docs.title: Colored Output
docs.text:
DBWarden now displays colored output for better readability:

  • [PENDING] - Yellow status for pending migrations
  • [APPLIED] - Green status for successfully applied migrations
  • [ROLLED_BACK] - Red status for rolled back migrations
  • SQL keywords are highlighted in magenta
  • SQL strings are highlighted in green
  • SQL comments are highlighted in cyan

docs.title: Migration Dependencies
docs.text:
Specify dependencies between migrations using the header:

-- depends_on: ["0001", "0002"]

-- upgrade

CREATE TABLE posts (...);

-- rollback

DROP TABLE posts;

DBWarden will resolve dependencies and execute migrations in the correct order.

docs.title: Seed Data Migrations
docs.text:
Mark migrations as seed data using the -- seed marker:

-- seed

-- upgrade

INSERT INTO service_types (name, kind) VALUES
('Web Service', 'api'),
('Database', 'db');

-- rollback

DELETE FROM service_types WHERE name IN ('Web Service', 'Database');

Seed migrations run after all versioned migrations.

docs.title: Baseline Migrations
docs.text:
Mark an existing database as already migrated without executing SQL:

dbwarden migrate --baseline --to-version 0001

This is useful when: - Starting with an existing database - Integrating DBWarden into an existing project - Recovering from migration tracking issues

docs.title: Backup Before Migration
docs.text:
Automatically create a backup before running migrations:

dbwarden migrate --with-backup
dbwarden migrate --with-backup --backup-dir /path/to/backups

Backups are stored in the backups/ directory by default.

docs.title: Migration File Types
docs.text: Prefix Type Behavior NNNN_*.sql Versioned Run once, in sequential order RA__*.sql Runs Always Run on every migrate execution ROC__*.sql Runs On Change Run when file checksum changes

docs.title: Versioned Migrations (NNNN_*.sql)
docs.text: Sequential migrations that run once in order: - 0001_create_users.sql - 0002_add_posts.sql - 0003_create_comments.sql

docs.title: Runs-Always Migrations (RA__*.sql)
docs.text: Repeatable migrations that run every execution: - RA__create_audit_views.sql - RA__seed_reference_data.sql

docs.title: Runs-On-Change Migrations (ROC__*.sql)
docs.text: Migrations that only run when their checksum changes: - ROC__update_config.sql - ROC__add_triggers.sql
